_description: Pairwise drug-drug interactions for CheckDrugDrugInteraction. Each pair is class-vs-class (or specific drug vs class) with severity, mechanism, and clinical action. Curated subset of the most clinically significant interactions from BNF Appendix 1.
_source: British National Formulary (BNF), Appendix 1 — interactions. https://bnf.nice.org.uk/interactions/

pairs.mechanism: NSAIDs antagonise the antihypertensive effect of ACE inhibitors and synergistically reduce GFR (renal afferent vasoconstriction + efferent vasodilation), increasing risk of acute kidney injury, particularly with concomitant diuretic.
pairs.action: Avoid combination if possible. If unavoidable, monitor renal function and U&Es weekly during initiation.
pairs.citation: BNF Appendix 1 — NSAIDs / ACE inhibitors interaction

pairs.mechanism: Same mechanism as NSAID + ACE-I — antagonism of antihypertensive effect and increased AKI risk.
pairs.action: Avoid; monitor renal function if combination unavoidable.

pairs.mechanism: NSAIDs blunt the natriuretic effect of loop and thiazide diuretics and synergistically reduce renal perfusion.
pairs.action: Avoid combination if possible. Monitor renal function and fluid status if used together. Note: when combined with an ACE-I or ARB the triad is the 'triple-whammy' (see DDI-NSAID-DIURETIC-ACEI), upgraded to severe.
pairs.citation: BNF Appendix 1 — NSAIDs / loop diuretics interaction

pairs._note: TRIPLE-DRUG rule — fires only when an NSAID, a diuretic, AND an ACE-I/ARB are ALL on the active list. Evaluator must respect requires_all_classes.
pairs.mechanism: Triple-whammy: NSAID + diuretic + ACE-I/ARB markedly increases risk of acute kidney injury, especially in older adults or with CKD. Cumulative renal hit (volume depletion, blocked compensatory vasoconstriction, blunted prostaglandin-mediated perfusion).
pairs.action: Avoid combination. If absolutely necessary, monitor renal function within 72 hours of initiation and weekly thereafter.
pairs.citation: BNF Appendix 1 / NICE NG203; Lapi F et al. BMJ 2013;346:e8525

pairs.mechanism: NSAIDs increase bleeding risk via platelet inhibition and gastric mucosal damage; some NSAIDs also displace warfarin from protein binding raising INR.
pairs.action: Avoid combination. If unavoidable, gastroprotection (PPI), close INR monitoring.

pairs.mechanism: Additive bleeding risk (especially GI). NSAIDs increase risk of GI ulceration; DOACs reduce clotting.
pairs.action: Avoid combination. Use paracetamol where possible; if NSAID essential, gastroprotection and short course only.
pairs.citation: BNF Appendix 1 — NSAIDs / direct-acting oral anticoagulants

pairs.mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal clearance of methotrexate, raising serum levels and risk of bone marrow suppression and mucositis. Risk highest with high-dose methotrexate.
pairs.action: Caution with low-dose weekly methotrexate; counsel patient to monitor for mouth ulcers, infection, bruising. Avoid with high-dose oncology methotrexate.

pairs.mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal clearance of lithium; serum lithium can rise rapidly into toxic range.
pairs.action: Avoid combination. If unavoidable, check lithium level within 5 days of starting NSAID and weekly thereafter.

pairs.mechanism: Additive GI bleeding risk (SSRIs reduce platelet serotonin, NSAIDs damage gastric mucosa).
pairs.action: Co-prescribe PPI gastroprotection; consider paracetamol alternative.

pairs.mechanism: Macrolides (especially clarithromycin, erythromycin) inhibit CYP3A4 and gut flora producing vitamin K, raising INR.
pairs.action: Check INR within 3-5 days of starting macrolide; reduce warfarin dose proactively or use azithromycin if appropriate.

pairs.mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, markedly increasing warfarin exposure and INR.
pairs.action: Check INR within 3-5 days; reduce warfarin dose proactively (often 25-50% reduction).

pairs.mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A4. Effect builds over weeks due to long amiodarone half-life.
pairs.action: Reduce warfarin dose by approximately 25-50% on initiation of amiodarone; check INR weekly for 6 weeks.

pairs.mechanism: Clarithromycin and erythromycin inhibit CYP3A4, markedly raising simvastatin/atorvastatin levels and risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.
pairs.action: Withhold simvastatin/atorvastatin during macrolide course (or use azithromycin which is less inhibitory).
pairs.citation: BNF Appendix 1 — statins / macrolides; MHRA Drug Safety Update

pairs.mechanism: Additive risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis (especially with gemfibrozil).
pairs.action: Avoid gemfibrozil + statin. Fenofibrate + statin acceptable with patient counselling on muscle symptoms.

pairs.mechanism: Diltiazem/verapamil inhibit CYP3A4 raising statin levels and myopathy risk.
pairs.action: Limit simvastatin to 20mg daily; consider switching to pravastatin or rosuvastatin (not CYP3A4 metabolised).

pairs.mechanism: Grapefruit inhibits intestinal CYP3A4 increasing AUC of simvastatin/atorvastatin and myopathy risk.
pairs.action: Counsel patient to avoid grapefruit juice while on simvastatin/atorvastatin; pravastatin/rosuvastatin not affected.

pairs.mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits P-glycoprotein, reducing digoxin clearance; serum digoxin can roughly double.
pairs.action: Halve digoxin dose on amiodarone initiation; check digoxin level 7 days later.

pairs.mechanism: Verapamil inhibits P-glycoprotein and reduces renal digoxin clearance; risk of toxicity.
pairs.action: Reduce digoxin dose by 25-50% on verapamil initiation; monitor levels.

pairs.mechanism: Loop diuretic-induced hypokalaemia/hypomagnesaemia potentiates digoxin toxicity.
pairs.action: Monitor K+ and Mg2+; replace deficits.

pairs.mechanism: Both raise serum K+ — additive risk of hyperkalaemia, particularly with CKD or in older adults.
pairs.action: Check K+ within 1 week of starting/titrating; clinically appropriate combination in HFrEF but requires monitoring.
pairs.citation: BNF Appendix 1 — ACE inhibitors / aldosterone antagonists; NICE NG106

pairs.mechanism: Additive hyperkalaemia.
pairs.action: Avoid combination unless K+ documented low and monitored.

pairs.mechanism: Trimethoprim acts as a potassium-sparing diuretic; additive hyperkalaemia, especially in older adults with CKD.
pairs.action: Avoid combination if alternative antibiotic available; if used, check U&Es within 5 days.
pairs.citation: BNF Appendix 1 — ACE inhibitors / trimethoprim; MHRA Drug Safety Update Sept 2014

pairs.mechanism: Dual RAAS blockade increases hyperkalaemia, hypotension and AKI risk without survival benefit.
pairs.action: Avoid except in specialist-supervised refractory heart failure.
pairs.citation: BNF Appendix 1 — ACE inhibitors / angiotensin receptor blockers; MHRA Drug Safety Update May 2014

pairs.mechanism: Risk of life-threatening serotonin syndrome.
pairs.action: Contraindicated. Allow 2-week washout (5 weeks for fluoxetine).

pairs.mechanism: Additive serotonergic effect — risk of serotonin syndrome and lowered seizure threshold.
pairs.action: Avoid where possible; counsel patient on serotonin syndrome symptoms.

pairs.mechanism: Theoretical risk of serotonin syndrome (clinically rare).
pairs.action: Counsel; combination acceptable in most patients.

pairs.mechanism: See DDI-NSAID-SSRI — additive GI bleeding risk.
pairs.action: Co-prescribe PPI; consider paracetamol alternative.

pairs.mechanism: Risk of contrast-induced AKI causing metformin accumulation and lactic acidosis.
pairs.action: Withhold metformin from time of contrast administration; recheck eGFR at 48 hours; resume if renal function unchanged.
pairs.citation: BNF — metformin (precautions); RCR / Royal College of Radiologists guidance

pairs.mechanism: Excessive alcohol potentiates metformin-associated lactic acidosis.
pairs.action: Counsel against binge drinking; review if hazardous use.

pairs.mechanism: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 and P-gp, raising colchicine levels into toxic range — risk of pancytopenia, myopathy, multi-organ failure.
pairs.action: Avoid combination. If essential, suspend colchicine for course of macrolide.

pairs.mechanism: Ciclosporin inhibits CYP3A4 and P-gp, raising colchicine to toxic levels.
pairs.action: Avoid; if unavoidable, reduce colchicine dose markedly and monitor for toxicity.

pairs.mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9, raising phenytoin levels.
pairs.action: Reduce phenytoin dose; monitor levels.

pairs.mechanism: Macrolides inhibit CYP3A4, raising carbamazepine to toxic levels.
pairs.action: Avoid clarithromycin/erythromycin; use azithromycin if possible.

pairs.mechanism: Additive QT prolongation; risk of torsades de pointes.
pairs.action: Avoid; if essential, baseline ECG and electrolyte correction.

pairs.mechanism: Additive QT prolongation. Citalopram itself has QT-prolonging properties.
pairs.action: Avoid combination with other QT-prolonging drugs (e.g. amiodarone, methadone, quinolones).
pairs.citation: BNF — citalopram (cautions); MHRA Drug Safety Update Dec 2011

pairs.mechanism: See DDI-WARFARIN-AMIODARONE.
pairs.action: Reduce warfarin 25-50%; weekly INR.

pairs.mechanism: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 and P-gp, raising DOAC levels (especially apixaban, rivaroxaban).
pairs.action: Use azithromycin if alternative needed; if combination essential, monitor for bleeding and consider dose reduction.

pairs.mechanism: Rifampicin induces CYP3A4 and P-gp, reducing DOAC levels and risking thrombosis.
pairs.action: Avoid; switch to LMWH/warfarin during rifampicin course.

pairs.mechanism: ACE-I reduces lithium clearance, raising serum lithium.
pairs.action: Check lithium level within 5-7 days of starting ACE-I.

pairs.mechanism: Thiazides reduce lithium clearance, can double serum levels.
pairs.action: Avoid combination if possible; if required, halve lithium dose and check levels weekly.

pairs.mechanism: Omeprazole/esomeprazole inhibit CYP2C19, reducing conversion of clopidogrel to active metabolite — possible reduced antiplatelet effect.
pairs.action: Use pantoprazole or lansoprazole if PPI required, or use H2 antagonist.
pairs.citation: BNF Appendix 1 — clopidogrel / PPIs; MHRA Drug Safety Update April 2010

pairs.mechanism: Some reports of raised INR. Less marked than clarithromycin/erythromycin.
pairs.action: Check INR mid-course or 3-5 days post.

pairs.mechanism: Both inhibit folate metabolism; risk of bone marrow suppression and pancytopenia.
pairs.action: Avoid combination. Use nitrofurantoin or alternative for UTI in MTX patients.

pairs.mechanism: See DDI-NSAID-METHOTREXATE — reduced clearance.
pairs.action: Caution at low-dose weekly MTX (rheumatology); avoid with high-dose oncology MTX.

pairs.mechanism: Additive respiratory depression and sedation; significant overdose mortality.
pairs.action: Avoid combination. If essential (palliative care, opioid-tolerant), use lowest doses and counsel on overdose risk.
pairs.citation: BNF — opioids and benzodiazepines (cautions); MHRA Drug Safety Update March 2020

pairs.mechanism: Additive negative inotropy and AV-node block; risk of severe bradycardia, heart block, or asystole.
pairs.action: Avoid IV combination; oral combination only with specialist supervision.

pairs.mechanism: Additive negative inotropy and AV node depression.
pairs.action: Use cautiously; monitor HR and BP.

pairs.mechanism: See DDI-DIGOXIN-AMIODARONE.
pairs.action: Halve digoxin; check level day 7.

pairs.mechanism: Both raise K+; risk of hyperkalaemia.
pairs.action: Avoid; use alternative antibiotic.

pairs.mechanism: Disulfiram-like reaction (flushing, vomiting) — historical association though clinical magnitude debated.
pairs.action: Counsel patient to avoid alcohol during course and 48h after.

_clinical_review_note: Each entry summarises a named clinical source in original wording (fair use). clinical_review_status='summarised_from_named_source' means: source is named, mechanism/severity reflect the source's substance, but verbatim text is NOT reproduced. NICE/MHRA-cited entries may be upgraded to 'verbatim_verified' (those sources are Open Government Licence). BNF/Beers/STOPP-START stay summarised indefinitely (closed-licence sources).
